1: Chapter 16 introduces one of the most striking aspects of Jeremiah's prophetic vocation: he is commanded not merely to speak judgment but to embody it in his own life. The word of YHWH came to me — the chapter contains three divine prohibitions that will make Jeremiah a living sign-act before the people.
2: You shall not take a wife, nor shall you have sons or daughters in this place. The prohibition of marriage and family is unprecedented in the prophetic literature. The symbolic logic is direct: in a city under judgment, bringing children into the world is an act of hope; Jeremiah's childlessness enacts the hopelessness of Jerusalem's immediate future. In a culture where having descendants was the primary form of social immortality and covenant continuity, celibacy was a form of public mourning. Jeremiah's unmarried state is a walking sermon.
3: For thus says YHWH concerning the sons and daughters who are born in this place, and concerning the mothers who bore them and the fathers who fathered them in this land. The command is grounded in what is about to happen to children born in Jerusalem. The verse does not specify the disaster but makes clear that children born now will face a fate worse than their parents'. Jeremiah's refusal to have children is not selfish but compassionate: he is being spared the anguish of watching his own children suffer the coming destruction.
4: They shall die of deadly diseases. They shall not be lamented, nor shall they be buried. They shall be as dung on the surface of the ground. They shall perish by the sword and by famine, and their dead bodies shall be food for the birds of the air and for the beasts of the earth. The catalogue of horrors — disease, no lamentation, no burial, desecration as dung, sword, famine, bodies consumed by birds and beasts — is comprehensive. The absence of lamentation and burial means the deaths will not even receive the minimal dignity of communal mourning. This is covenant curse at its most extreme, recalling Deuteronomy 28:26.
5: For thus says YHWH: Do not enter the house of mourning, or go to lament or grieve for them, for I have taken away my peace from this people, my steadfast love and mercy, declares YHWH. The second prohibition: Jeremiah must not participate in mourning rites. The house of mourning (beit marzeach — possibly connected to the marzeach institution, a communal feast associated with death and ancestors) was the normal context for communal grief. His absence from mourning symbolizes the withdrawal of divine compassion: I have taken away my peace, my steadfast love and mercy (shalom, chesed, rachamim) — the three covenant attributes YHWH withdraws from a people who have exhausted his patience.
6: Both great and small shall die in this land. They shall not be buried, and no one shall lament for them or cut himself or make himself bald for them. The mourning practices mentioned — cut himself and make himself bald — were prohibited for Israelites in Leviticus 19:28 and Deuteronomy 14:1, yet were clearly practiced in some form as grief expressions (cf. Jeremiah 41:5; Amos 8:10). The absence of even these (prohibited but culturally present) mourning gestures indicates a disaster so total that normal grief protocols collapse. When everyone is dead, there is no one to mourn the dead.
7: No one shall break bread for the mourner, to comfort him for the dead, nor shall anyone give him the cup of consolation to drink for his father or his mother. The provision of food and drink to mourners was a practical form of communal solidarity — bereaved families who could not cook for themselves were fed by neighbors. This meal of consolation (lechem anashim, bread of men) is attested in ancient Near Eastern contexts as a fundamental obligation of community. Its absence signals not merely poverty but the collapse of the social bonds that make community possible.
8: You shall not go into the house of feasting to sit with them, to eat and drink. The third prohibition: Jeremiah must not participate in communal celebrations either. He is barred from mourning (v5) and from feasting (v8) — excluded from the full range of communal life. His isolation is total and deliberate. The prophet who cannot mourn with mourners or celebrate with celebrants is a visible rupture in the social fabric, a walking sign that normal communal life is about to end.
9: For thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Behold, I will silence in this place, before your eyes and in your days, the voice of mirth and the voice of gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride. The silencing of wedding celebration is a specific covenant-curse indicator. The voice of the bridegroom and the bride appears again in Jeremiah 7:34; Jeremiah 25:10; Jeremiah 33:11 — in 33:11 its return marks the restoration of covenant joy. Its silencing here marks the suspension of normal life. Wedding songs were the most public expression of communal hope; their absence signals the death of hope.
10: And when you tell this people all these words, and they say to you, 'Why has YHWH pronounced all this great evil against us? What is our iniquity? What is the sin that we have committed against YHWH our God?' The anticipated question from the people is one of self-justifying incomprehension: they genuinely do not see the connection between their behavior and the announced disaster. This is not entirely disingenuous — generations of religious syncretism had normalized practices that YHWH condemned. The question provides Jeremiah an opportunity to deliver the full covenant indictment.
11: Then you shall say to them: Because your fathers have forsaken me, declares YHWH, and have gone after other gods and have served and worshiped them, and have forsaken me and have not kept my law. The diagnosis is generational: the fathers began the apostasy. The present generation has inherited a corrupted religious environment. Forsaken YHWH… gone after other gods… forsaken YHWH… not kept my law — the double forsook (azavuni) forms a rhetorical bracket emphasizing abandonment as the core offense.
12: And you have done worse than your fathers, for behold, every one of you follows his stubborn, evil will, refusing to listen to me. The present generation is not merely the passive recipient of ancestral sin — they have intensified it. Every one of you follows his stubborn, evil will (sherirut lev hara' — recurring Jeremianic phrase): the hardened disposition has become universal and individual. The aggravating factor — you have done worse than your fathers — removes the possible defense that the people were simply caught in inherited patterns.
13: Therefore I will hurl you out of this land into a land that neither you nor your fathers have known, and there you shall serve other gods day and night, for I will show you no favor. The punishment fits the crime with covenantal precision: they chose to serve foreign gods while in the covenant land; they will serve foreign gods in a foreign land — but under compulsion rather than choice, and without any prospect of the divine favor they currently enjoy in Canaan. The exile is a perverse fulfillment of their own desires: you wanted foreign gods, you will be immersed in them.
14: Therefore, behold, the days are coming, declares YHWH, when it shall no longer be said, 'As YHWH lives who brought up the people of Israel out of the land of Egypt'. A remarkable intrusion of restoration hope into the judgment section. The Exodus was Israel's foundational confession — the formative act of divine redemption that defined YHWH's character and Israel's identity. To say that a new act of redemption will supersede it in memory is an extraordinary theological claim. The new event will be so significant that the Exodus — the reference point for all divine saving action — will recede from primary position.
15: But 'As YHWH lives who brought up the people of Israel out of the north country and out of all the countries where he had driven them.' For I will bring them back to their own land that I gave to their fathers. The new Exodus will be a return from the north country — Babylon — and from all the nations of the diaspora. The scope is explicitly global: all the countries where he had driven them. This is not merely a local restoration but a worldwide regathering. The New Testament understands this promise through the lens of Christ's gathering of the scattered children of God from all nations (John 11:52; Ephesians 2:11–22).
16: Behold, I am sending for many fishermen, declares YHWH, and they shall catch them. And afterward I will send for many hunters, and they shall hunt them from every mountain and every hill, and out of the clefts of the rocks. The fishermen and hunters in the context of vv14–15 are the agents of regathering — those who will seek out the scattered diaspora and bring them back. Some interpreters read these as instruments of judgment (hunting them down for punishment), others as agents of restoration. The context of the new-Exodus promise favors restoration: the scattered will be actively sought and returned.
17: For my eyes are on all their ways. They are not hidden from me, nor is their iniquity concealed from my eyes. YHWH's omniscience is the guarantor both of judgment (nothing is hidden from the one who judges) and of restoration (no exile is too remote to be found and gathered). The dual application of divine sight — seeing sin for judgment and seeing the scattered for restoration — is a characteristic of biblical covenant theology: the same attribute that makes YHWH a terrifying judge makes him a faithful redeemer.
18: But first I will doubly repay their iniquity and their sin, because they have polluted my land with the carcasses of their detestable idols, and have filled my inheritance with their abominations. The double-repayment (mishne) is the legal penalty for aggravated theft in Exodus 22:4, 7 — paying back twice what was stolen. Applied to covenant violation, it means the people will experience the full consequences of their apostasy compounded. The land-pollution framing is significant: idols are described as carcasses (nivelat) — dead bodies contaminating the ground — a term of maximum ritual impurity applied to objects the people treated as sacred.
19: O YHWH, my strength and my stronghold, my refuge in the day of trouble, to you shall the nations come from the ends of the earth and say: Our fathers have inherited nothing but lies, worthless things in which there is no profit. The nations' future confession of the worthlessness of their inherited gods echoes and extends the idol-polemic of chapter 10. The phrase from the ends of the earth makes this a truly universal eschatological vision: every people, having inherited religious systems built on falsehood, will abandon them for YHWH. The nations' self-condemnation (our fathers have inherited lies) mirrors Israel's own indictment throughout the book.
20: Can man make for himself gods? Such are not gods! The rhetorical question captures the core epistemological absurdity of idol-worship: a manufactured object cannot be a god. The nations will eventually recognize what Israel should have known all along. The verse is structurally a coda to the idol-polemic sequence that began in chapter 10 and resurfaces throughout chapters 14–16.
21: Therefore, behold, I will make them know, this once I will make them know my power and my might, and they shall know that my name is YHWH. The chapter closes with a declaration of divine self-revelation through judgment. They shall know that my name is YHWH — the recognition formula, prominent throughout Ezekiel, signals that the purpose of the coming events (judgment, exile, restoration) is ultimately revelatory: YHWH is making himself known to both Israel and the nations through what he is doing in history. The new-Exodus events will produce covenant knowledge where previously there was only habitual religious indifference.
